Here
div.formula: M + b+0 = 1 N q e iqb+ W ,(196)
M + b+b ′ + = 1 N q 2 cos (qb ′ + ) e iqb+ W ,(197)
W = |E| -α cos q x -β cos q x 2 cos √ 3q y 2 -γ sin q x 2 sin √ 3q y 2 ,(198)
div.p: and α, β, and γ are defined in Eqs. (C.3)-(C.5). Integration over BZ can be replaced with integration over the rectangle 0 ≤ q x ≤ 2π, 0 ≤ q y ≤ (4π)/ √ 3. The expressions for M + are given in Appendix C.2. The following relations hold for any P:
Additional relations hold along BZ symmetry lines.

In the triplet sector, there are three anti-symmetrized vectors: {b -} = {1, 2, 3}. Performing the same variable change, q ′ = q -P 2 , and amplitude redefinition Φb-= e -i(P/2)b-Φ - b-, the master equations yield
where
div.formula: M - b-b ′ - = 1 N q (-2i) sin (qb ′ -) e iqb- W .(202)
div.p: The expressions for M -are also given in Appendix C.2, Eqs. (C.38)-(C.46). One has:

Thus, the triplet dispersion is described by a symmetric matrix for all P. A typical pair dispersion is shown in Fig. 17. Of note is the fact that attraction V = -16 t is barely above the pairing threshold for the highest, f , state given by Eq. (217). Thus, all six pair states are well defined in the entire BZ. At weaker attractions, the bound states begin to disappear into the two-particle continuum one-by-one. The effective mass of the lowest, s-symmetric pair is shown in Fig. 18. One can observe that the mass indeed approaches the strong-coupling limit [134][135][136], m * p /m 0 = 6, for all U . However, the approach is slow. For most realistic attractions, |V | < 20 t, the pair mass is no heavier than just 4 free-particle masses.

div.head: Pairing thresholds at the Γ-point
div.p: Determination of pairing thresholds from the general dispersion relations, Eqs. ( 193) and ( 201), at arbitrary P can be done only numerically. At the Γ-point, the systems acquire additional symmetries and the thresholds can be derived analytically. Analysis is greatly aided by the group theory. All the necessary information is given in Appendix C. 4.
For the singlet states, we combine Φ 0 , which is unchanged, with symmetrized combinations of basis functions given by Eqs. (C.60) and (C.63) into a new basis:
In terms of the new basis, dispersion equation, Eq. ( 193), transforms into a block-diagonal form:

(205) The top-left 2 × 2 block describes an s-symmetric ground state. To find the binding condition, set E → -12 t, at which all M + diverge logarithmically. To obtain a finite result, introduce the differences:
Next, express M + via L + and M + 00 and expand the 2 × 2 determinant. The (M + 00 ) 2 term vanishes identically while the coefficient at M + 00 must be zero. This leads to a binding condition
Analytical expressions for L + are given in Appendix C.3. The final result reads
The other two 1×1 blocks in Eq. ( 205) describe a d-symmetric dublet. Direct calculation yields the pairing threshold
A transformed dispersion equation reads
The first two blocks describe a p-symmetric doublet, whereas the lower-right block describes one f -symmetric state.

Note that both M - 11 and M - 12 converge at threshold, and no subtraction procedure is necessary. Starting with Eqs. (C.38) and (C.39), setting α = 4t, β = 8t, γ = 0, E = -12t, elementary integration yields
Consequently, the pairing thresholds are

div.head: 3D. U V model on the simple cubic lattice
div.p: One qualitatively new feature of 3D models is a larger role of kinetic energy. A finite attraction is needed to form a pair even at U = 0. The U V model on the simple cubic lattice was solved in Ref. [11,102]. In this section, those results are rederived and extended using the (anti)-symmetrized method developed here.

div.head: Singlet states
div.p: The symmetrized set of vectors consists of four elements: {b + } = {(0, 0, 0), (1, 0, 0), (0, 1, 0), (0, 0, 1)}. Similar to the square lattice, Eqs. ( 27) and ( 28) are transformed by changing integration variables, q ′ j = q j -Pj 2 , and functions, Φ+
x = e -i(Px/2) Φ + 100 , and so on. The resulting 4 × 4 linear system reads
div.formula: M nmk = π -π π -π π -π dq x dq y dq z (2π) 3 cos nq x cos mq y cos kq z |E| -α cos q x -β cos q y -γ cos q z ,(219)
div.p: where α = 4t cos (P x /2), β = 4t cos (P y /2), and γ = 4t cos (P z /2). Pair dispersion is obtained by equating the 4 × 4 determinant to zero. The quantities M nmk are generalized Watson integrals. For arbitrary P, they can be computed numerically. On BZ diagonals, including the Γ point, α = β = γ. In this case, all M nmk in Eq. ( 218) can be expressed via the complete elliptic integrals in closed form. The expressions are given in Appendix D.1.

The upper-left corner describes an s-symmetric ground state. Remarkably, all the matrix elements can be expressed via the basic integral M 000 :
Expanding the 2 × 2 determinant, the s-pair dispersion equation becomes

From Eq. ( 224), pair energy can be found numerically for any given U , V , and α. Let us determine the binding threshold along BZ diagonals. To that end, set |E| = 3α. The numerical value of M 000 at this energy was given in Eq. (86). It can be written as

The full phase diagram of the simple cubic U V model is shown in Fig. 19.

div.head: Triplet states
div.p: The anti-symmetrized set of vectors consists of three elements: {b -} = {(1, 0, 0), (0, 1, 0), (0, 0, 1)}. The dispersion equation splits into three independent equations describing three p-symmetric pairs:
This critical value is plotted in Fig. 19.

div.head: 3D. Tetragonal U V model
div.p: In the tetragonal U V model, attractive potential V z and hopping integral t z along z axis differ from their xy counterparts, see Fig. 20. The two extra parameters bring considerable richness and complexity. The tetragonal model smoothly interpolates between the quasi-2D limit t z ≪ t, V z ≪ V (Section 5) and the quasi-1D limit t z ≫ t, V z ≫ V (Section 4) via the isotropic 3D case (Section 8). The quasi-2D sector is most relevant to the physics of high-temperature superconductors, as discussed in the Introduction. From that standpoint, the special case of 0 ≤ t z ≤ t and V z = 0 was analyzed in Ref. [32]. It was argued that both t z and V have non-monotonic effects on preformed-pair superconductivity. A small V cannot form pairs whereas a large V produces pairs that are too heavy. In both cases, superconductivity is suppressed. (This may help understand why the highest T c occurs at intermediate electron-phonon coupling in HTSC [139].) Likewise, a large t z destroys the pairs because large kinetic energy overcomes a moderate V . In the opposite limit of very small t z , pairs lose 3D coherence, z-axis mass becomes very large, and the condensation temperature drops. Thus, it was argued, preformed pair superconductivity is optimal at intermediate values of V /t and t z /t.

In this section, the general case of nonzero V z is considered. Due to the model's complexity, very few results can be derived analytically. Bulk of the results presented below are obtained by solving pair dispersion equations numerically.
div.formula: x y z +U -V -V -t -t z -t z -t -V z

div.head: Singlet states. Pairing thresholds at the Γ-point
div.p: Derivation of pair dispersion proceeds along the same lines as the simple cubic case of Section 8. The result [32] is again Eq. ( 218) in which the last column contains V z instead of V . A second difference concerns the expression for integrals M nmk , Eq. ( 219): the parameter γ is now given by γ = 4t z cos (P z /2). We note in passing that replacing V with another parameter V y in the third column of Eq. ( 218) and setting β = 4t y cos (P y /2) in Eq. ( 219) results in a dispersion relation for the orthorhombic U V model. The latter is not studied in this paper.

We begin with analysis of the binding conditions at the Γ point where M 100 = M 010 , M 200 = M 020 , and M 101 = M 011 . Instead of doing a full point symmetry analysis, it is easier to proceed by observing that taking a sum and a difference of the second and third equations in Eq. (218) splits off one d-symmetric state. The difference can be written in terms of Φ +
x -Φ + y :

This equation describes a d x 2 -y 2 -symmetric solution with a threshold that smoothly interpolates from the isotropic cubic case, Eq. ( 228), to the pure 2D limit, Eq. ( 160), shown in Fig. 22. The other three equations are

This system describes a mixture of one s-symmetric and one d-symmetric states. Upon setting E = E 11 = -8t-4t z , the consistency condition of Eq. ( 234) links four model parameters: U , V xy , V z and t z . One of them can be expressed via the other three. A new feature of this model relative to the cases considered before is the ability to tune the degree of 3D anisotropy. Therefore, we will be mostly interested in t z -dependence of binding conditions. It is convenient to expand the determinant in Eq. ( 234) in powers of U and V . This is done in Appendix D. 4. From here, one potential can be expressed via the other two. Out of all possibilities, we consider three special cases: (A) (B) V z = 0. In-plane attraction only. Set V z = 0 and expand the remaining 2 × 2 determinant in Eq. ( 234) to express V xy,cr vs. U : 235). In the tz → 0 limit, the model reduces to the square U V model. The tz = 0 value is given by Eq. ( 154) and is marked by a circle. (C) Out-of-plane attraction only. The dot-dashed line is Eq. ( 236). In the tz → 0 limit, the model reduces to the 2D attractive Hubbard model with zero binding threshold. The square marks the binding threshold for out-of-plane attraction (zero). Inset: comparison of cases B and C at extreme anisotropy.
div.formula: V z = V xy , (B) V z = 0,
div.p: In the 2D limit, t z → 0, all M nmk logarithmically diverge, but utilizing a subtractive procedure M nmk = M 000 + L nmk , one can show that Eq. ( 235) reduces to Eq. ( 154).
(C) V xy = 0. Out-of-plane attraction only. Set V xy = 0 and expand the remaining 2 × 2 determinant in Eq. ( 234) to express V z,cr vs U :

Both Eqs. ( 235) and (236) mark the appearance of an s-symmetric pair. In case B, the pair is "disk-like" extending in the xy-plane. In case C, the pair is "cigar-like" extending along z-axis. Both functions are plotted in Fig. 21. It is instructive to compare cases B and C at different degrees of anisotropy. At t z /t = 1, hopping is fully isotropic and all V 's contribute equally to binding. There are four attractive bonds in case B but only two in case C. Therefore, one expects the case C threshold to be larger, which can be observed in Fig. 21. However, in the opposite 2D limit, t z → 0, the situation is reversed. Case B with in-plane attraction reduces to the square U V model studied in Section 5. Accordingly, the threshold line terminates at a U -dependent finite value given by Eq. ( 154). (The limit is marked by a circle in Fig. 21.) In contrast, in case C two particles reside on adjacent z planes and attract each other when their xy coordinate coincide. Thus, the model reduces to the 2D attractive Hubbard model where the threshold is zero, see Section 3.3. The C threshold line extends all the way to |V | = 0, which is marked by a square in Fig. 21. The zero threshold can also be deduced from Eq. ( 236). In the t z → 0 limit, both M 001 and M 002 tend to zero rather than logarithmically diverge. The threshold reduces to the attractive Hubbard expression |V cr | = 1/M 000 . Since M 000 diverges, the threshold is zero. The entire line V z (t z ) in Fig. 21 almost exactly matches the one in the tetragonal attractive Hubbard model, see Section 3.6 and Fig. 5(a). The effects of Hubbard repulsion U are barely felt. By continuity, the B and C threshold lines must cross, which can be seen in the inset of Fig. 21. The crossing happens at high anisotropy, t z ≃ 0.002 t. This is a useful anisotropy scale that separates two different regimes. At t z < 0.002 t, pair formation is driven by logarithmic divergencies of M 's. This is where pairs can be considered purely two-dimensional. At t z > 0.002 t, the divergencies are no longer dominant, and pairs become three-dimensional yet strongly anisotropic. This point will be discussed further in Section 12.

div.head: Triplet states. Pairing thresholds at the Γ-point
div.p: There are three spin-triplet pairs, all with p-type orbital symmetries. Their dispersion relations are given by Eqs. ( 229)-( 231). The only difference is that V xy should be used in the Φ -

x and Φ - y equations but V z in the Φ - z equation. Like in the simple cubic case, decomposition into three independent dispersion relations takes place over the entire BZ of pair momenta. In this section, we only investigate binding conditions at the Γ point. To that 158), and the isotropic 3D limit, Eq. ( 228). Similarly, the px, py line interpolates between Eq. ( 169) and Eq. ( 232). The dashed line is Eq. ( 237).

end, we set α = β = 4t, γ = 4t z and E = -8t -4t z . Analysis of the p x and p y thresholds, which are equal, is straightforward. They smoothly interpolate between the pure 2D limit, Eq. ( 169), and the isotropic 3D limit, Eq. ( 232). A numerical solution of Eq. ( 229) for all t z /t is plotted in Fig. 22.

The situation with the p z state is more interesting. Although Eq. ( 231) contains a difference of two M 's suggesting convergence at t z → 0, M 002 actually tends to zero while M 000 still diverges, resulting in a zero threshold. A numerical solution of Eq. ( 231) plotted in Fig. 22 confirms the conclusion (thick solid line). Due to a relative simplicity of Eq. ( 231), it is possible to derive an asymptotic behavior of |V pz z,cr | in the t z → 0 limit. This is done in Appendix D.5. The result is
div.formula: V pz z,cr (t z → 0) ≈ 8πt ln 32 t √ e tz . (237
div.p: This formula is plotted in Fig. 22 as the dashed line and is in excellent agreement with the exact result for t z /t < 0.1. Combining this result with the discussion of s-pair formation in Section 9.1, one concludes that two bound states are formed with zero thresholds in case (C), one cigar-shaped s and one p z . This is an unusual situation in U V models since normally pairs of different symmetries are formed at different thresholds. The tetragonal U V model with out-of-plane attraction is richer than the attractive Hubbard model.

div.head: Bose-Einstein condensation of real-space pairs
div.p: The tetragonal U V model is a convenient system to discuss Bose-Einstein condensation (BEC) of real-space pairs. BEC lies at the core of the preformed-pairs mechanism of high-temperature superconductivity [11,13,16,30,32,36]. One should also mention BEC of molecules engineered in cold gases [76,140,141]. However, BEC is a many-body effect and as such is out-of-scope of this work. For that reason, the following discussion is only qualitative.

It was argued in Section 9.1 that if t z > 0.002 t, then pairs are already three-dimensional yet highly anisotropic. The above condition is satisfied in most crystalline solids. Hence, the pairs' collective behavior should be described in terms of anisotropic 3D BEC [13,15] rather than a pure 2D Berezinskii-Kosterlitz-Thouless transition. A recent neutron scattering study [142] supports this viewpoint.
A convenient starting point is the anisotropic version of the continuous-space BEC formula:
div.formula: k B T BEC = 3.31 2 ñ 2 3 p ( m * 2 px m * pz ) 1 3 . (238
div.p: Here, ñp and m * p are the density and effective mass of bosons (fermion pairs) in physical units. Transitioning to relative units one obtains
div.formula: T BEC ≡ 1 t k B T BEC = 6.62 n 2 3 (m * 2 px m * pz )1 3
div.p: (239) Here, n is the number of pairs per unit cell, and the pair masses are expressed in units of m 0 = 2 /(2ta 2 ). We also assume m * px = m * py . As was argued in the Introduction, a maximum critical temperature is reached at "closepacking" when the pair density is approximately equal to the inverse pair volume, n cp = Ω -1 p . The close-packing temperature is:
div.formula: T * BEC = T BEC (n cp ) = 6.62 (Ω 2 p m * 2 px m * pz )1 3
div.p: This formula contains only single pair properties supplied by the exact solution. The continuum approximation can be superseded by a more rigorous lattice treatment. The pair density is computed as a full Bose integral and then equated to an inverse pair volume. Instead of Eq. ( 240), one has BZ d 3 P (2π) 3 1 exp E(P)-E0
E P is the full pair dispersion also provided by the exact solution. This equation [32] does not resort to the effective mass approximation.

Here, we apply the simplified formula, Eq. ( 240), to the tetragonal U V model with out-of-plane attraction only. Only s-symmetric pairs are included. The effective masses are calculated numerically from the full singlet dispersion relation [take Eq. ( 218) and set V = 0 in the two middle columns]:
For the pair volume, we adopt the following formula [32]:

which also defines the pair sizes r * px , r * py , and r * pz . This form of Ω p respects the exclusion principle and ensures the pair volume is not less than 1 even in the strong coupling limit. The mean-squared distances are defined via the full coordinate wave function, Eq. (18). For example

In taking the momentum sum in Eq. ( 18), k 1 = -k 2 should be used, which corresponds to P = 0. In principle, all the sums can be computed by brute-force numerical calculation utilizing FFT. However, for the tetragonal dispersion, (△r i ) 2 can be analytically reduced to ratios of two one-dimensional integrals (see Ref. [32], Appendix C), which makes calculation of Ω p much more efficient. Figure 23 shows the effective pair mass and radius as functions of t z for U = 30 t and V z = 5 t. For this attraction, the pair exists in the interval 0 < t z /t < 0.205, see Fig. 21, case (C). One expects the pair to be strongly bound at t z → 0 and loosely bound at t z → 0.205 t. This can be seen in the effective radius plots: both r * px and r * pz tend to 1 in the 2D limit but diverge near the threshold. The in-plane pair mass, m * px , is weakly affected by out-plane anisotropy and stays almost constant as a function of t z . In contrast, the out-of-plane mass strongly depends on t z . Near the binding threshold, it approaches the z-mass of two free particles, 2t/t z . In the opposite limit, t z → 0, it diverges as ∝ V z /t 2 z . Now consider the close-packed BEC temperature given by Eq. ( 240). It is bounded by two divergencies: a divergent m * pz at t z → 0 and a divergent Ω p near the threshold. Thus, we expect T * BEC to have a maximum at an intermediate t z . This is shown in Fig. 24. The present model predicts the existence of an optimal degree of anisotropy. If t z is too large, the kinetic energy is large, pairs are loosely bound, their volume is large, the packing density is small, and T * BEC is small as a result. If t z is too small, the the out-of-plane mass is very large and T * BEC is small again. A similar conclusion was reached in Ref. [32] for in-plane attraction. Another interesting feature of Fig. 24 is that the peak value of T * BEC increases with the attraction strength. A stronger V z creates more compact pairs which boosts the close-packed density. However, large V z 's cause phase separation as discussed in Section 11.

div.head: Light pairs in the strong coupling limit
div.p: We have already discussed various aspects of light bound pairs in the 1D chain (Section 4.2), in the 2D square lattice (Section 5.4), and in the 2D triangular lattice (Section 7). In this section, we study several other systems that host mobile pairs in the strong-coupling limit.

div.head: Staggered square planes
div.p: Consider a lattice consisting of two square lattices shifted out-of-plane and staggered in the (xy) plane relative to each other, see Fig. 25(a). In the limit of very strong out-of-plane attraction, one particle will reside on the first plane and another particle on the second plane. The magnitudes of U and inter-plane hopping t z are irrelevant. At the same time, the bound pair can still move in the first order in in-plane hopping t. In the strong-coupling limit, there are four non-equivalent dimer configurations shown in the figure . Because the particles cannot exchange, there is no need to consider symmetrized basis states. Acting on the dimer states by the Hamiltonian yields: Next, compose a Schrödinger equation and transform it to momentum space. That results in a consistency condition

10.2. Square U V model with resonant attraction, U = V The simple square U V model with only nearest-neighbor attraction does not support light pairs. However, light pairs become possible for resonant attraction U < 0, U = V . In this case, the energy of on-site dimer and nearest-neighbor dimer are equal, and the pair can move in the first order in t. Figure 25(b) shows the three relevant basis dimers. Since the particles can exchange, it is essential to consider symmetrized states:
The Hamiltonian action is
div.formula: ĤA m = - √ 2 t (B m + B m-x ) - √ 2 t (C m + C m-y ) ,(253)
div.p: Notice the special role of dimer A: it is coupled to both B and C while the latter two are not coupled directly but only through A. That will allow for generalization to other lattices to be discussed in Section 10.3. The dispersion relation reads

div.p: This simple form enables analytical expressions for several other lattices. For example, in the 3D simple cubic lattice, b + = x, y, and z. The lowest energy band is

div.p: The effective mass is
Comparison between Eqs. ( 134), (257), and (261) suggests an obvious generalization to hyper-cubic lattices in higher dimensions D > 3.
In the body-centered cubic lattice, there are four vectors: b + = 1 2 (x ± y ± z). Dimer dispersion has five energy bands with Ẽ2,3,4 = 0 and Ẽ1,5 = ±(4 t) 1 + cos

div.p: The effective mass of the lowest band is
It is noteworthy that Eqs. ( 135), ( 258), (262), and (264) can be unified as

where z is the number of nearest neighbors in respective lattices. 125) and ( 154), respectively. Quad thresholds V 4 were obtained by extrapolating to infinite lattices. In 2D, the U → ∞ limit of V 4 is ≈ 3.35 t, which is consistent with the 4 × 4 values reported in [33,34]. The dot-dashed line in panel (b) is the pair threshold in the 3D tetragonal U V model with Vz = 0 and tz = 0.01 t, see Eq. (235).

div.head: More than two particles
div.p: The properties of one bound pair, which this review summarizes, make physical sense only if the pairs keep their identity in a macroscopic system with finite particle density. For most of the results to remain valid, the pairs should not aggregate in trions, quads, and larger clusters. In other words, the effective interaction between pairs should be either repulsive or weakly attractive when such a weak attraction is unable to bind pairs into larger complexes (in 3D). The situation is clear in attractive Hubbard models. Two fermions forming an on-site real-space pair have two opposite spin projections and prevent other fermions from occupying the same site at the same time. Thus, clusters of three or more fermions are prohibited by the exclusion principle. The attractive Fermi-Hubbard model is stable against phase separation [143], and all the results derived in Section 3 remain valid. Bosons, on the other hand, can pile up on the same site without limits, sending the total energy to negative infinity. The attractive Bose-Hubbard model is unstable beyond the pair-forming threshold, such as the ones listed in Table 1.

The situation is more subtle in U V models, t-J models, and other models where the attractive interaction is of finite range. The exclusion principle does not directly prohibit pairs from sticking to each other side-by-side and forming a macroscopic cluster. Thus, any such system will phase-separate in the large V limit. However, the exclusion principle still plays an important role at intermediate V . In the context of large bipolarons, Emin argued [144] that when forming a quadpolaron, additional carriers must occupy excited states of the self-trapping well. That leads to an additional short-range repulsion between pairs (large bipolarons in this case). A liquid of real-space pairs must be stable against clustering in a finite attraction interval just above the pairing threshold. Thus, the question becomes quantitative: when exactly does the system phase-separate, and how is the phase separation threshold V ∞ related to the pair-forming threshold V 2 studied in this paper? Since the discovery of high-temperature superconductivity, these issues have been discussed mostly in the context of phase-separation in the t-J model [33,34] and in bipolaronic superconductors [57,58]. Those investigations were based on comparing the energy of a collection of pairs with the energy of an infinite cluster. Because both energies were determined either by applying approximations or on small lattice segments, the phase boundaries were approximate.

Rigorous determination of clustering thresholds and phase boundaries requires solving a few-body or a full many-body problem on an infinite lattice with sufficient accuracy, and is therefore difficult. Berciu reported [145] a zero region of pair stability in a system of 3 to 5 spinless fermions with nearest-neighbor attraction in an infinite 1D lattice. However, with second-nearest-neighbor repulsion turned on, the pairs stabilized in a wide interval of parameters. In this model, pairs need to be stabilized dynamically because Emin's argument does not apply to spinless fermions. Also in 1D, Chakraborty, Tezuka and Min investigated four "extended-Holstein bipolarons" on chains up to 24 sites long [146]. At high phonon frequencies, this model [137] maps to a U V model. The authors reported a narrow but finite interval of bipolaron stability in qualitative agreement with the 1D U V phase diagram shown on Fig. 26(a) and to be discussed below.

In 2D, Emery, Kivelson, and Lin reported [33] pair stability in the interval 2.0 t < |V | < 3.53 t for a 4 × 4 lattice and U = ∞. A similar interval, 2.0 t < |V | < 3.8 t on a 4 × 4 lattice, was reported by Dagotto and Riera for the t-J model [34].
Multi-magnon bound states in a 2D square frustrated magnetic were analyzed in [86].
We are unaware of any numerical investigation of clustering in 3D lattices except our own work [147] to be discussed next.

We now summarize our own results on pair liquid stability in U V models [35,[147][148][149]. First off, bosonic U V models are unstable: as soon as pairs form, trions [148] and quads [35] form as well, triggering phase separation. The same conclusion applies to spinless fermions: those pairs are unstable, too. Phase diagrams of four spinful fermions in 1D and 2D are shown in Fig. 26(a) and Fig. 26(b), respectively [35]. In 1D, the pair stability region is narrow; it shrinks to zero in the U → ∞ limit. In 2D, the pair stability region is ≈ 2t wide for all U . In 3D, exact solutions are computationally more demanding. Only a three-fermion problem has been solved in a 3D U V model so far [147]. Figure 27 shows the phase diagram of three fermions in the tetragonal U V model for U = 10 t. Pairs are stable against formation of trions between the solid and dashed lines. One important feature of Fig. 27 is the sharp increase of V 2 for small but nonzero t z . Going back to Fig. 26(b), one should be careful when interpreting the pure 2D case as an approximation to a highly anisotropic 3D case. Even a small t z elevates the V 2 line and significantly reduces the domain of pair stability, which is illustrated by the t z = 0.01 t line. Although the domain of pair stability shrinks significantly upon turning on interlayer hopping, the former remains finite for all U .

A high-level conclusion from this analysis is the existence of finite domains of pair stability in various forms of the U V model in all lattice dimensionalities. It is within such domains is where the results derived in the bulk of this paper are valid. The fundamental reason for pair stability is the Fermi statistics and associated nodes in the many-fermion wave functions [35,144]. Since these reasons are general and applicable to any system, we expect them to hold for other types of potentials including, for example, large-radius attractions considered in Section 5.4.

div.head: Common properties of lattice bound pairs
div.p: In this paper, U V models (extended attractive Hubbard models) of increasing complexity have been analyzed in detail. Taken together, the results point to several common properties of lattice bound states. They are summarized in this section.

div.head: Mathematically exact solutions
div.p: The most important property of two-particle lattice problems is their exact solvability. This is a big advantage as all the results can be easily verified and cross-checked, both analytically and numerically. When it comes to physical applications, typical arguments are centered around justifications of a starting Hamiltonian or relevancy to a particular real system. But once the model is agreed upon, the conclusions that follow are not usually questioned.

At the same time, the complexity of a solution increases rapidly with interaction range and lattice dimensionality. As shown in Section 2.2, the size of a matrix that defines pair dispersion, Eq. ( 17), is equal to the number of interacting lattice sites. Separation into singlet and triplet states described in Sections 2.3 and 2.4 cuts the matrix size by about half, which is helpful. Still, the ability to derive analytical results is largely limited to nearestneighbor interactions. The longer-range square model considered in Section 5.4 and Ref. [99] is a rare exception. Additionally, the matrix elements M that populate Eq. ( 17) are two-body lattice Green's functions whose complexity increases with lattice dimensionality. In general, M 's can be analytically evaluated in 1D, see Eqs. ( 65) and (C.18)-(C.22), and in 2D, see Appendix B. In 3D, analytical expressions are only known for the isotropic simple cubic lattice, Appendix D.1. In other cases, numerical integration is necessary. The ability to perform two integrations analytically, as shown in Appendix D.3, reduces M 's to one-dimensional integrals which makes subsequent numerics efficient.

The exact dispersion equation defines pair energy over the entire BZ of pair momenta. It provides singlettriplet splitting, energy gaps between various sub-bands of the same parity, and a total bandwidth. The dispersion possesses degeneracies at high-symmetry points, which can be cross-checked with group theory classifications. By setting the pair energy equal to the lowest energy of two free particles with the same total momentum a binding threshold is determined. At this special energy, matrix elements M simplify considerably. In many cases, and especially at the Γ point and along BZ diagonals, the thresholds can be calculated analytically. Many explicit examples have been derived in this paper.

The exact solution also provides pair effective mass and radius. The mass is rigorously defined via a second derivative of the total energy near the band minimum and as such it can be extracted from the dispersion relation.

In some cases, one can utilize the fact that the dispersion relation simplifies along BZ diagonals and derive analytical expressions. See, for example, Es. (77). Calculation of effective radius is more involved as it requires knowledge or the entire pair wave function and its subsequent integration. Still, analytic expressions have been obtained in simple cases, see Eqs. ( 71) and (80). In more complicated models, analytical manipulations enable reductions of multidimensional integrals to one-dimensional integrals, as was done, for example, in Ref. [32]. The effective radius yields the pair volume and the close-packed density of pairs which is an important parameter in the theory of Bose-Einstein condensation of pairs. Together with pair mass and full dispersion, it provides estimates of the close-packed BEC temperatures and enables conclusions about optimal model parameters.

div.head: Pair binding energy depends on its momentum
div.p: This is purely a lattice effect that is absent in continuous space [1]. Since the lattice provides a reference frame, movement through it is not Galilean-invariant. Binding energies and masses of bound complexes become dependent on their momenta P. The pair energy and the minimum energy of two free particles both rise with P but the latter rises faster. It means that the binding energy increases with P. In systems with a finite threshold, it leads to situations when there are no bound pairs at P = 0 but one or more bound pair at finite P. A similar effect has long been known in the theory of quantum spin waves [79]. Increasing stability of pairs can also be seen in threshold formulas with explicit dependence on P, see for example Eqs. ( 125), (159), and (226), where thresholds decrease to zero in BZ corners. That implies there is always at least one bound pair in BZ corners for any U as long as |V | > 0.

An important consequence is the existence of a binding surface in momentum space which separates unbound and bound pairs. As shown in Section 5.2, the binding surface does not in general match the Fermi surface of free electrons. The mismatch leads to segmentation of the Fermi surface into areas that are more or less prone to pairing and potential appearance of Fermi arcs. We note that such a pair would have formed with a nonzero total momentum. This is opposite to conventional Cooper pairs that are formed with zero total momentum. The described effect may also have links to the FFLO phase. A key question is how the binding surface is modified by a finite fermion density. This is a many-body problem that goes beyond the scope of this work.

div.head: Light pairs
div.p: Effective mass is an important characteristic of a bound pair. Broadly, it defines pair mobility and response to external perturbations. In the context of superfluidity, the mass determines a BEC temperature in a system of many pairs, as argued in the Introduction. It is important to know what makes pairs either light or heavy.

Near threshold, the mass is always close to two free-particle masses. In the opposite, strong coupling limit, the mass generally scales as m * p /m 0 ∝ |V |/t, i.e., increases linearly with the binding energy. This is because the pair moves in the second order in hopping t and breaks an attractive bond in the intermediate state, see Fig. 8(a). However, there are two circumstances when a pair can move in the first order in t and the above scaling is not followed. Instead, pair mass remains of order m 0 at all energies including the strong coupling limit. We refer to such pairs as light.

The first situation arises for purely geometric reasons. One pair member can hop to a neighbor site without breaking an attractive bond. In other words, both the starting site and the ending site are nearest neighbors to the second pair member. Then the second member moves in the same way and the process repeats. The constituent particles hop in turns and the entire pair moves through the lattice in a "crab-like" fashion without ever breaking a bond. A good example is provided by the triangular lattice investigated in Section 7.1 (see also Refs. [136,138]). Pair movement is illustrated in Fig. 16 and the mass is plotted in Fig. 18. Observe that even at extreme attractive strengths, |V | > 60 t, the pair mass remains below 5m 0 . For more realistic attractions, |V | < 10 t, the mass is below 3m 0 . That is, mass increase due to binding is less than 50%. It was suggested in Ref. [138] in the context of bipolaron superconductivity that for that very reason triangular and hexagonal lattices may host high-temperature superconductivity. Other lattices that support crab motion and light pairs include staggered ladders [57,134,135] (m * p < 4m 0 ), staggered square planes (Section 10.1, m * p < 4m 0 ), and face-centered cubic lattice [90] (m * p < 6m 0 ). One should add to this list the body-centered tetragonal lattice that supports light pairs in xy plane but not in z direction. This lattice is of interest because of potential connection to cuprate superconductivity. Light pairs in this lattice are yet to be studied. Inclusion of second-neighbor hopping induces light pairs in other lattices [58,133,134].

The second situation leading to light pairs arises when two or more lattice sites have equal attractive potentials. One such model was considered in Section 4.2. If the on-site interaction is attractive rather than repulsive and U = V , then a first-order resonant motion is possible as illustrated in Fig. 8(b). The analysis is readily generalized to other lattices that do not support light pairs for geometric reasons, see Sections 10.2 and 10.3. The pair effective mass is m * p < 2 √ z m 0 , where z is the number of nearest neighbors in a lattice. Such a potential is unlikely to arise in a solid crystal but can be engineered in cold gases where U and V are controlled independently [69][70][71][72]. Another class of potentials involves equal-strength attractions at finite separation between particles. We considered a 1D example in Section 4.2, see Fig. 8(c), and a 2D example in Section 5.4. In both cases, m * p < 4 m 0 was found. One might think that such conditions require fine-tuning and is therefore unlikely in real systems. However, as was argued in Section 5.4, realistic potentials are combinations of decaying repulsions and long-range attractions. They necessarily have broad and shallow attractive minima. In those situations, the likelihood of two sites near the minimum to have equal attractive strengths is quite high. More instances of this mechanism are investigated in Ref. [128].

An overall conclusion of this analysis is that in many circumstances, binding does not lead to significant increase of pair mass. Pair mass is not an impediment for a high mobility or a high BEC temperature.

div.head: Anisotropic 3D pairs
div.p: The role played by lattice anisotropy is important in the context of cuprate superconductivity. All cuprate superconductors have a layered structure which has led to a popular point of view that the superconductivity is essentially two-dimensional. A number of pure 2D theoretical models have been proposed and extensively studied. In the preformed pair mechanism, however, superconductivity is still considered three-dimensional albeit highly anisotropic. Investigations conducted in the present work support this picture. We analyzed two 3D anisotropic models: tetragonal attractive Hubbard model in Section 3.6 and tetragonal U V model in Section 9. We found in that in both cases the pair formation line splits in two regions, see Figs. 5(a) and 21. At extremely high anisotropy, t z < 0.002 t, pair formation is indeed dominated by logarithmic divergencies of 2D integrals. By that measure, the pairs may indeed be regarded as pure two-dimensional. However, when t z > 0.002 t the divergencies are no longer dominant and the threshold is on the order of its value in an isotropic 3D lattice. For that reason, real-space pairs in this regime are already three-dimensional. Thus, two-body solutions may help to clarify the nature of superconductivity depending on the level of anisotropy observed in a system.

div.head: Stability of fermion pairs against phase separation
div.p: Phase separation is a ubiquitous feature of all models with finite-range attraction such as U V or t-J models. Physical reasoning suggests that any system with a strong enough attraction will form clusters and eventually phase-separate. It raises the question about stability of real-space pairs in many-body systems. A positive answer is provided by the Fermi statistics [35,144,[147][148][149]. The ground state of a fermion pair is usually a spin singlet with a nodeless wave function. When two pairs attempt to coalesce into a quad, the full wave function must develop nodes. It is equivalent to a short-range repulsion between pairs. This additional repulsion keeps the pairs separate as long as attraction is not too strong. (Since this reasoning does not apply to bosons, Bose-U V models are not stable against phase separation [35].)

The derived conclusion is very general and applicable to a wide class of inter-particle potentials including threedimensional and long-range ones. It has been confirmed by exact numerical calculations for t-J models [33,34], U V models [35,[147][148][149], and electron-phonon models [146]. Real-space pairs retain their identity in a finite interval of attraction above the binding threshold.

x = 1 -n |V|/t t t 0 t h 0 1 pairing threshold optimal doping close packing Figure 28: Evolution of a U V model with hole doping. The carrier hopping (right axis) increases from t h near half-filling (x = 0) to t 0 in the empty lattice (x = 1). Accordingly, the attraction strength expressed in units of t (left axis) decreases from above threshold to below threshold.

div.head: Relevance to cuprate superconductivity
div.p: Real-space pairs are known to adequately describe many aspects of high-T c phenomenology. This topic is not discussed here and the reader is referred to available reviews [11-13, 15, 21]. We also note the recent observation of pairs in copper oxides [19] and iron-based superconductors [17,18].

In this section, we discuss insights into cuprate superconductivity provided by two-body problems. The first one concerns the role of anisotropy. It was shown in Sec. 9.3 and Ref. [32] that the preformed pair mechanism naturally leads to the notion of optimal anisotropy. If t z is large, then kinetic energy is large, attraction is below the threshold, and pairs do not form. Even if attraction is above the threshold, the binding is weak, pairs are large, close-packed density is small and T * BEC is small as a result. In the opposite limit of very small t z , pairs are well formed and are compact but the out-of-plane mass is very large, which also reduces T * BEC . Thus, there must be an optimal t z . Importantly, this argument does not exclude the existence of fully isotropic 3D superconductors like Ba 1-x K x BiO 3 . Rather, it means that any isotropic superconductivity with low carrier density can be improved upon by reducing coupling between layers. That will result in more compact pairs and a higher T * BEC . We also note, in passing, that the level of anisotropy can be dramatically altered by electron-phonon interaction [150] or similar mediators.

The above argument applies equally to quasi-1D superconductivity where the crystal is composed of weakly bound 1D chains. In this respect, the recent claim of a T c = 90 K superconductivity in a quasi-1D copper oxide is particularly noteworthy [151].

Similar to anisotropy, there is an optimal strength of attraction. A weak attraction cannot form pairs but a strong attraction leads to clustering and phase separation. Aggregating available results on the tetragonal U V model [32,147], one finds that a "working" interval of |V | is about 0.5 t. The interval is actually U -dependent, see Fig. 26(b). It grows larger at small U but shrinks at very large U > 50 t. Then an optimal level of anisotropy [147] is t z < 0.1 t. Let us assume t = 0.1 eV as a ballpark estimate of the in-plane hopping in physical units. (A corresponding total bandwidth is ≈ 1 eV.) Then the working interval of attraction is △V ≈ 0.05 eV and the optimal interlayer hopping is t z < 0.01 eV. Thus, the levels of attraction and anisotropy must both fall within narrow intervals to be close to optimal. This may help explain the experimental fact that high-T c superconductivity is a rare phenomenon only found in a few materials families.

Further insights can be gained by examining Fig. 24. It clearly shows that the peak T * BEC increases with |V |. It is beneficial for superconductivity to increase |V | all the way until phase separation. One concludes that highest critical temperatures are expected in systems close to phase separation. Since the system is close to phase separation globally, it may go over the threshold locally due to disorder, lattice defects, Jahn-Teller distortions [23,152], and other factors. This may help explain proliferation of charge density waves, nematic orders, and other charge instabilities experimentally observed in the cuprates [153][154][155][156][157].

Finally, we discuss the role played by strong electron correlations represented by Hubbard repulsion U . Consider the square lattice with bare electron hopping t 0 . Near half-filling, the effective hopping of holes is reduced by strong correlations by a factor of ∼ 4 -10 [117,129]. As a consequence, a relatively weak attraction |V | that was unable to bind fast electrons moving with hopping t 0 is now able to bind slow holes moving with a smaller effective hopping t h . The role of strong in-plane correlation is to further slow down the carriers to enable pair formation by a weak attraction. In a recent analysis of thermodynamic measurements of several cuprate families, Harrison and Chan argued [130] that the in-plane hopping near half-filling is about t h = 0.11 eV while it should be 0.36 eV according to electronic structure calculations. Such a band narrowing is due to electron correlations. Since the band structure value should be accurate in an empty lattice, one concludes that the effective t increases from 0.11 to 0.36 eV with doping. At the same time, quantum chemistry calculations [131,132] suggest an attractive potential of about 0.12 eV. Thus, V /t ≈ 1 near half-filling. This may be sufficient for binding in some highly anisotropic U V models.

Consider evolution of the system with hole doping starting at half-filling illustrated in Fig. 28. By continuity, the effective carrier hopping must smoothly increase from t h at x = 0 to t 0 at x = 1. Since exact shape of this curve is unknown, we show it schematically as a straight line. We then assume that the attraction strength in physical units stays approximately constant with doping. Then, |V | will be systematically decreasing when expressed in units of t. If |V | was above a pairing threshold at x = 0, it will end up below the threshold at a finite x. At this doping, the pairs disappear. At some intermediate doping, the pairs balloon in size to reach close-packing. At this optimal doping, the critical temperature is maximal. The preformed-pair mechanism naturally explains the existence of optimal doping and eventual disappearance of superconductivity with increasing hole density. A recent analysis of the magnetic susceptibility and Knight shift in cuprates [158] suggests that the pseudogap decreases linearly with doping and coexists with superconductivity until both disappear in the overdoped regime. This conclusion supports our qualitative scenario. Returning to the specific numbers reported in Ref. [130], let us assume the threshold to be |V cr | = 0.6 t. When t increases to ≈ 0.20 eV with doping, the pairs would evaporate. Thus, superconductivity should disappear by x = 0.36.

div.head: Future outlook
div.p: Several additional two-body problems seem to be of interest. One of them is the body-centered tetragonal (BCT) U V model with out-of-plane attraction. The BCT lattice supports in-plane light pairs (but not out-of-plane ones), which should boost T * BEC . The negative effects of U on pairing can be minimized by a small t z . Compared with the simple tetragonal lattice, BCT has four times as many attractive bonds, which should reduce the threshold values by about a factor of four. Finally, BCT is close to the crystal structure of some cuprate superconductors.

It would also be interesting to analyze two-body problems with effective single-particle dispersions arising from strong correlations. For example, holes in nearly half-filled t-J models acquire the tendency to hop diagonally to second nearest neighbors [117,129]. According to Bak and Micnas [37], such a dispersion may result in a dsymmetric ground state. Thus, such models may shed light on the symmetry of superconducting order parameter in cuprates.

Also of interest are multi-orbital models describing CuO 2 planes and even full CuO 6 octahedra. Only one such investigation has been published so far [104]. Extension of this work to 3D and accommodation of the available quantum-mechanical calculations of t, U , and V [131,132,159,160], would make the theory less phenomenological.
Finally, we mention the need to investigate a four-fermion problem in the tetragonal U V model to complete the picture of phase separation in 3D.

Thus, M sq 10 also contains a special case of Π(-κ, κ). However, we previously derived an expression for M sq 10 that includes K(κ) only.

Several comments are in order. (i) M sq nm (E; α, α) describe not only bound U V pairs in the Γ point but on the Brillouin zone diagonal as well. This follows from the definitions of α and β. (ii) All integrals have the analytical structure of αM sq nm = Q 1 (κ -1 )K(κ) + Q 2 (κ -1 )E(κ) + Q 3 (κ -1 ), where Q 1,2,3 are polynomials and κ = 2α |E| . The smallest power in all Q i is ≥ -1 while the highest power grows with n and m. The overall complexity of the analytical expressions increases with n and m. All M sq nm include a nonzero term ∝ K(κ) that logarithmically diverges when E → -2α -0, as expected from the definition, Eq. (B.1). However, coefficients by K(κ) approach a universal limit 1 πα that is the same for all n and m. As a result, all differences M sq nm -M sq n ′ m ′ are finite at E = -2α. They are discussed in the next section.

Appendix B.4. M sq nm -M sq 00 at threshold. Square case, α = β As discussed in the main body of this paper, to determine a pairing threshold, energy E must be sent to the lowest energy of two free particles from below. For the isotropic square U V model, it means E → -2α -0. As shown in Appendix B.4, all M sq nm logarithmically diverge in that limit and direct determination of the threshold is impossible. The procedure can be regularized by subtracting from all M sq nm a common term that has the same logarithmic divergence. The subtrahend can be any one of M sq nm or any other function that has the same logarithmic divergence. The most obvious choice is M sq 00 . Accordingly, we introduce the following new functions
div.formula: L sq nm (α, α) ≡ M sq nm (E = -2α; α, α) -M sq 00 (E = -2α; α, α) , (B.38)
div.p: that are finite for all n, m and α. It can be deduced from the definitions, Eq. (B.1) and Eq. (B.38), that all L sq nm < 0. There are two principle ways of calculating L sq nm . For those {nm} for which full expression M sq nm is known, L sq nm is given by its non-singular part, utilizing E(1) = 1. But in general, L sq nm can be calculated directly from the definition, Eq. (B.1), because in this special case the second integration is also elementary. Below, we list L sq nm for |n| + |m| ≤ 5.

div.p: dq x dq y (2π) 2 e i(-qx 2 +qy) sin -qx 2 + q y |E| -α cos q x -β cos qx 2 cos q y -γ sin 193) and (201), are quite complex and in general can be solved only numerically. However, at high symmetry points of the Brillouin zone there is enough simplification that some results can be derived analytically. The analysis is greatly aided by the theory of point groups. This section is devoted to the exposition of this subject. The triangular lattice is used as an example; U V models on other lattices can be analyzed similarly.

At the Γ-point (P = 0), the following relations among singlet matrix elements hold: 201) acquire additional symmetries. It is clear that a bound pair at P = 0 should possess a hexagonal symmetry, which implies the solutions can be classified according to the irreducible representations (irreps) of the point group C 6v . We now execute a textbook algorithm [170] of constructing linear combinations of functions Φ that form bases of the irreps of C 6v .

The starting point is the three basis functions Φ + 1-3 in the singlet case (Φ 0 will be added later) and Φ - 1-3 in the triplet case. By acting on both bases by the symmetry operations of C 6v , see and so on. The characters of D ± are given in Table C.2. Applying the orthogonality theorem, one obtains the decompositions The matrices D for both E 2 and E 1 are given below. For a fixed index k, application of Eq. (C.62) yields two linear combinations (one for i = 1 and another for i = 2) that together form a basis sought. Thus, each irrep gets two bases (one for k = 1 and one for k = 2), one of which may be trivial. In the case of E 2 , the k = 2 basis turns out to be trivial (identical zero) whereas k = 1 produces a nontrivial basis
(C.64) This completes the analysis of symmetry at the Γ point. Equations (C.61) and (C.64) are combined to form a new basis, Eq. ( 212). The matrices of E 2 are:
The matrices of E 1 are:

Joyce [96] also derived an explicit expression for M sc 300 and provided an algorithm how to systematically derive M sc nmk from M sc 000 , M sc 200 and M sc 300 using several recurrence relations. Thus, any M sc nmk can be analytically expressed with products of the complete elliptic integrals K and E, at least in principle.

At threshold, |E| = 3α, all M sc nmk can be expressed via M sc 000 using the remarkable identities derived by Glasser and Boersma [93] and Joyce [96] M sc 200 (3α) = M sc 020 (3α) = M sc 002 (3α) = - cos nq x cos mq y cos kq z |E| -α(cos q x + cos q y ) -γ cos q z . (D.14)
As far as we are aware, only the basic integral M tg 000 has been evaluated analytically for arbitrary |E|, α, and γ by Delves, Joyce and Zucker [94,95,97]: 15) where

Compared with the original papers, we have applied the identity cos nq x cos mq y cos kq z |E| -α cos q x -β cos q y -γ cos q z , (D.18) apply to the simple cubic U V model at arbitrary P, tetragonal U V model (t x = t y = t z ) at arbitrary P, and orthorhombic U V model (t x = t y = t z , not studied in this paper) also at arbitrary P. They cannot be evaluated analytically. However, two out of three integrations can be carried out analytically. Thus, only one integration remains to be done numerically which leads to an efficient numerical scheme. The first integration in Eq. (D.18) is elementary while the second produces complete elliptic integrals. First, we introduce three elliptic moduli: From here, one potential can be expressed via the other two.

figure.head: Figure 5 :
figure.figDesc: Figure 5: Properties of bound pairs in the anisotropic tetragonal attractive Hubbard model. (a) Binding threshold as a function of hopping anisotropy. (b) Anisotropy of effective masses. (c) Anisotropy of effective radii. Circles indicate threshold tz values for given |U |.

figure.head: Figure 6 :
figure.figDesc: Figure 6: Singlet-pair dispersion Es in the 1D U V model computed from Eq. (123) (thick solid line). U = 15 t, |V | = 1.3 t. Note that |V | is below the P = 0 binding threshold, 2U t/(U + 4t). The shaded area is the continuum of two-particle scattering states. The circles mark threshold momenta, P = ±2 arccos [U V /2/(U -2V )], derived from Eq. (125). The triplet pair energy Et given by Eq. (145) is very close to Es and therefore not shown in the figure.

figure.head: 2 Figure 7 :
figure.figDesc: Figure 7: Singlet pair mass in the 1D U V model computed from Eqs. (123) and(127). The thin solid line corresponds to a fixed U = 20 t. The mass grows linearly with |V |. The circle marks threshold value, Eq. (125), for P = 0. The thick solid line corresponds to the light pairs regime with U = -|V |. The mass stays of order m 0 for all V . At strong coupling, |V | → ∞, the mass approaches the theoretical limit, m * ps = √ 8 m 0 (the dashed line), see Eq. (135).

figure.head: Figure 8 :
figure.figDesc: Figure 8: Illustration of the light bound pair mechanism. (a) Conventional pair movement. The intermediate configuration has a larger energy, which results in a mass growing linearly with |V |. (b) In the resonant case, U = -|V |, there are intermediate configurations with the same energy as the initial and starting configurations. (c) An attractive potential with a flat section at a nonzero separation between two particles. In this case, V 3 = V 4 .

figure.head: Figure 9 :
figure.figDesc: Figure 9: Two-fermion phase diagrams in the 1D and 2D square U V models at P = 0.

figure.head: Figure 10 :
figure.figDesc: Figure 10: The pairing lines in the 2D square U V model for U = 30 t and |V | = 1.1 t. Bound pairs form outside the respective lines. The s and d lines are solutions of Eqs. (163) and(164). The px and py lines are solutions of Eqs. (167) and (168) at E = -α -β. At these parameters, both p and d lines terminate at the Brillouin zone boundaries.[87]

figure.head: Figure 11 :
figure.figDesc: Figure 11: Comparison of the pairing and Fermi lines in the 2D square U V model. The thick solid line is the same s pairing line drawn in Fig.10. The hatched area in the center are free-particle states enclosed by a Fermi line with energy E F = -2.7 t. It corresponds to a filling factor of 0.113 and total particle density of n = 0.226. The dashed line is the twice-Fermi momentum line. The four circular segments outlined by thick lines enclose free particle states that would tend to pair up if they were moving in an empty lattice.

figure.head: Figure 12 :
figure.figDesc: Figure 12: Dispersion of bound pairs in the 2D square U V model for U = 30 t and V = -14 t. The s and d energies are solutions of Eq. (148). The s dispersion along the X -M line is given by Eq. (165). px and py energies are solutions of Eqs. (167) and (168), respectively. Notice px, py degeneracy along the M -Γ line and s, px degeneracy along the X -M line.

figure.head: Figure 13 :
figure.figDesc: Figure 13: Binding threshold of the U V model on the square lattice versus the distance of nth nearest neighbors from the origin. U = ∞. The dashed line is a phenomenological dependence |Vcr| = 2/R 2 and is guide to the eye. Adapted from Ref. [99].

and (C) V xy = 0, all at a fixed U . (A) V xy = V z ≡ V . In this case, Eq. (D.34) becomes a quadratic equation for |V cr |. Two real roots correspond to the formation of two bound states: a low-energy one with s orbital symmetry and a high-energy one with (d xz + d yz ) symmetry. Both thresholds are shown in Fig. 21 as functions of t z . At t z /t = 1, this model is equivalent to the simple cubic U V model studied in Section 8.1.

figure.head: Figure 21 :
figure.figDesc: Figure 21: Binding thresholds of singlet pairs in the tetragonal U V model at the Γ-point and U = 30 t. Pairs are formed to the right of their respective lines. (A) The solid lines describe formation of an s-and a d-symmetric pair when Vxy = Vz. At tz/t = 1, their values are given by Eqs. (226) and (228), respectively. (B) In-plane attraction only. The dashed line is Eq. (235). In the tz → 0 limit, the model reduces to the square U V model. The tz = 0 value is given by Eq. (154) and is marked by a circle. (C) Out-of-plane attraction only. The dot-dashed line is Eq. (236). In the tz → 0 limit, the model reduces to the 2D attractive Hubbard model with zero binding threshold. The square marks the binding threshold for out-of-plane attraction (zero). Inset: comparison of cases B and C at extreme anisotropy.

The dot-dashed line marks the coordinates of peak close-packed BEC temperature, T * BEC , determined by locating the maxima of plots like those shown in Fig. 24.

div.p: The author wishes to thank James Hague for long-term collaboration and numerous discussions on the subject of this paper. The author also wishes to thank Alexander Chernyshev, Frank Marsiglio, and the anonymous referee for useful comments. div.head: Data availability
div.p: No data was used for the research described in the article.
In this section, we briefly describe other two-particle problems that have been left out of the present review.
Early results on bound pairs in 1D, 2D square, and 3D simple cubic U V models were derived by Micnas and reported in Sec. III.C of Ref. [11].

Two-particle bound states in the square t-J model and t-J-U models, which map to the U V model, were studied by Lin [60], Petukhov, Galán, and Vergés [61], and Kagan and Rice [62]. All those works are consistent with the material of Section 5.

Bak and Micnas [37,161] considered a square U V model with second nearest-neighbor hopping. They found that the latter could change the symmetry of the ground state. The d-symmetric pair had a lower energy than the s-symmetric one when the first-neighbor and second-neighbor hoppings were of opposite signs. Since effective second-neighbor hopping may be induced by strong correlations on an antiferromagnetic background near halffilling, this result may have implications for understanding symmetries of the superconducting order parameter in cuprates.
Marsiglio and Hirsch [124] developed and solved a two-body problem with correlated hopping, in which hopping of one particle depended on nearby presence of another particle.
In Ref. [162], a square U V model with anisotropic next-nearest-neighbor attraction was studied in the context of cold atom quantum simulator of extended Hubbard-Holstein models.
A two-magnon problem with complicated second-neighbor hopping and interaction was solved in Ref. [86].

A 3D U V model on the BCC lattice was considered in Ref. [89]. Pair mass, radius, and binding conditions were computed. Based on those results, a critical temperature of an atomic condensate in a BCC optical lattice was estimated to be around 10 nK.

In Ref. [90], a U V model on the FCC lattice was analyzed in the context of superconducting fullerides. The pairs were found to be of small radius, strongly-bound, but relatively light due to the light-pair effect. It was estimated that such pairs could Bose-condense at high temperatures even if the lattice constant was large, as in the fullerides.
The following works studied multi-orbital models with on-site or intersite attractions.

A "mixed" repulsive-attractive one-dimensional Hubbard model was studied in Ref. [103]. The unit cell consisted of two non-equivalent sites. The first site hosted a Hubbard repulsion U r whereas the second site hosted a Hubbard attraction -|U a |. The singlet pairs were found to form when

Note an unusual, square-root, dependence on the pair momentum P . The latter is confined to the interval -π 2 ≤ P ≤ π 2 in this case. In Ref. [39], a U V model on a checker-board square lattice was studied. The symmetry between two sublattices was assumed to be broken by antiferromagnetic correlations of the underlying many-body system. The correlations created a staggered magnetic field ∆ that elevated energy of a spin-up hole on the first sublattice and lowered it on the second sublattice. For a spin-down hole, sublattice energies were reversed. It was found that a nonzero ∆ favored pairing because of two effects. First, the resulting band splitting reduced kinetic energies of the holes. Second, a nonzero ∆ suppressed double occupancy and reduced the influence of U . Both factors effectively increased the attraction V relative to t and U , which leads to easier pairing. A more detailed analysis of this two-particle lattice problem can be found in Ref. [100].

Two-body bound states in multi-orbital models with flat bands were studied in Ref. [163]. Finally, we mention the mixed Hubbard model on a multi-orbital CuO 2 plane [104]. The model included a repulsion on copper sites, U C , and an attraction on oxygen sites, -|U O |. The atomic energies of Cu and O sites were assumed equal, ε C = ε O , for both spin orientations. The pairing condition was found to be

, where t is the absolute value of the hoping integral between the copper's d x 2 -y 2 orbital and oxygen's p x,y orbitals. One should add that multiple orbitals introduced significant technical complications in exact two-particle solutions, as explained in Appendix A.2. A similar analysis for the case of oxygen-oxygen attraction [164] resulted in a threshold condition, |V OO | > U C t/(0.595U C + 2t). The latter model in its many-body version was recently studied by constrained-path Monte Carlo in Ref. [51].

div.head: Appendix A.2. Extension to non-Bravais lattices and multi-orbital models
div.p: When two fermions interact within a complex lattice with S > 1 orbitals per unit cell, an exact solution becomes considerably more complicated. Systematic investigation of such models are beyond the scope of this work. In this section, we briefly outline the procedure and point out how it differs from the basic S = 1 case.

Assuming the index m continues to number unit cells, the two-body wave function Ψ αβ (m 1 , m 2 ) comprises S 2 components arranged in an (S 2 × 1) array. The Schrödinger equation, Eqs. ( 4) and (7), comprises S 2 coupled equations. The right-hand-side of Eq. ( 7) still contains a finite number of integrals Φ(P) which, similarly to Eq. ( 12), allows expressing ψ k1α,k2β as a linear combination of Φ(P). However, the respective energy-dependent coefficients are now components of an inverted (S 2 ×S 2 ) matrix. Each coefficient is a ratio of an (S 2 -1)-degree polynomial of E and an S 2 -degree polynomial of E. The denominator can be factorized into a product of S 2 factors (E -ε αk1 -ε βk2 ), where ε αk are the S bands of the single-particle dispersion. When ε αk is known analytically, factorization can also be performed analytically, at least in principle. Then each coefficient can be expanded into a sum of S 2 simple fractions. Finally, substitution of ψ k1α,k2β into the definitions of Φ(P) produces a finite set of algebraic equations for Φ(P) but with matrix elements M being sums of S 2 terms where each term has the general form of Eq. ( 15) with denominators (-E + ε αq + ε β,P-q ) and additional functions of q in numerators.

In practice, this program can be completed for the simplest multiorbital models only [39,100,103,104]. Often enough, ε αk cannot be calculated analytically, and the above procedure pauses after the matrix inversion but before factorization. From that stage, everything must be computed numerically. In this section, we will be concerned with analytical evaluation of the two dimensional integrals

Here α ≡ 4t x cos Px 2 ≥ 0 and β ≡ 4t y cos Py 2 ≥ 0. t x,y > 0 are absolute values of hopping integrals along x and y axes, respectively. P x and P y are two components of a total pair quasi-momentum. Since -π ≤ P x , P y ≤ +π, both α and β are non-negative. The quantities M rt nm (E; α, β) are Green's functions of the rectangular lattice. However, we prefer not to use the term "Green's function" in the main text of this study because our definition, Eq. (B.1), differs from the standard one by a constant negative factor. The superscript "rt" indicates that these M nm belong to the rectangular lattice. When α = β, a superscript "sq" will be used, indicating Green's functions of the square lattice. The rectangular functions, Eq. (B.1), appear in the square two-body problem because the center-of-mass motion breaks the x-y symmetry of the underlying lattice. We will be interested only in the discrete energy spectrum with E ≤ -(α + β). For those energies, all M rt nm are real, positive, and logarithmically diverge as E → -(α + β) from below. Since M rt -n,m = M rt nm and M rt n,-m = M rt nm , only M rt nm with non-negative n and m need to be evaluated. All M rt nm are eventually expressible via the three complete elliptic integrals K, E, and Π. In this paper, the following definitions are used
Expressions of M rt nm via the generalized hypergeometric function can be found in Ref. [165]. Rectangular Green's functions with second-neighbor hopping were evaluated in Ref. [161].

div.head: Appendix B.2. Basic integrals for the square case, α = β
div.p: We begin with calculating the basic integral M sq 00 for the isotropic case, α = β. First integration utilizes the residue theorem with the result

where z 1 = (|E| + α)/α and z 2 = (|E| -α)/α. Then two successive transformations of the integration variable . However, after that progress stops. Further extension of this method is impossible. Fortunately, there is a second, remarkable identity due to Morita [168], that involves only M n0 's and for our case reads

This expression is valid for n = 0. Using expressions for M sq 00 , M sq 10 , M sq 20 and M sq 30 derived above, one can validate Eq. (B.28) for n = 1. Then, by setting n = 2, 3, . . ., one can calculate M sq 40 , M sq 50 , and so on, for any n. After that, application of the sum rule, Eq. (B.7), enables calculation of any M sq nm , at least in principle. Below, we list all M sq nm for n + m ≤ 4.
It can be verified by direct substitution of the definitions, Eq. (B.1). The n = m = 0 version of the sum rule reads
which is satisfied by the expressions given above. By setting (n, m) = (1, 0) or (0, 1) in Eq. (B.59), one can derive the first diagonal integral
Using this representation, expressions for M rt 00 (E; α, β) and M rt 11 (E; α, β) can also be found in integrals handbooks (see, e.g., [169], # 2.15.20.1), albeit without derivation.
Appendix B.6. Recurrence relations for the rectangular case, α = β
To proceed further, we need anisotropic analogs of the Morita recurrence relation [168]. Repeating the derivation twice, first time for M rt n0 and second time for M rt 0m , one obtains

L rt nm (α, β) can be derived either by taking the E → -α -β limit in the general expressions for M rt nm (α, β), or by direct evaluation of the double integrals in definitions, Eq. (B.68). In the latter case, the second integration is elementary but can be laborious. Below, we list several L rt nm (α, β) for the lowest (n, m):

where the prime denotes derivative with respect to K's argument. Applying Eq. ( 76) of the main text, the last expression is rewritten as
